# Regional β Response & Immune-Niche Organization

## Abstract

Regional signal alone was insufficient: 50% of high-signal regions did not respond, whereas response coincided with immune-rich neighborhoods. The accepted analysis preserves $x_i \leq 1$ and a 25% threshold without changing the scientific argument.

## Introduction

Spatial context can condition regional measurements in heterogeneous systems [@doe2024]. Here, `response_rate` and signal intensity are treated as separate quantities rather than interchangeable evidence.

## Results

The response pattern followed local niche organization rather than signal alone (see @fig:regional). Productive red regions were adjacent to immune-rich gold neighborhoods, while blue regions remained non-responsive.

![Productive red regions lie next to immune-rich gold neighborhoods; blue regions are non-responsive.](../sdk-two-gate/studies/STUDY-002/output/figures/regional-response.ppm){#fig:regional}

### Quantitative summary

Table: Regional response & niche summary {#tbl:response}

| Group | High signal | Responded | Response rate |
|:--|--:|--:|--:|
| Immune-rich | 4 | 4 | 100% |
| Immune-poor | 2 | 0 | 0% |

The group-level contrast in @tbl:response does not establish causality. It records the accepted descriptive comparison with symbols μ, ±, and × preserved.

## Discussion

Together, the figure and table support a conditional association, not a universal rule or a new mechanism [@doe2024; @roe2023]. No claim is added during TeX rendering.

## Methods

The accepted story reports only the documented regional summary. Values with underscores such as cohort_A are escaped mechanically, while inline code `cohort_A` remains code.
